root.visit: August 627: Departed from Jangan
Qinzhou ( 秦州 ) with the monk Xiaoda ( 孝達 ) →
one-month stay in Guazhou ( 瓜州 ) near the border and
at the Yumen Pass 16 kilometers to the north; the governor of Liangzhou ordered his arrest, but Xuanzang passed the Yumen Pass at night with the help of Li Chang ( 李昌 )

→ attempted to cross a great desert around 320 kilometers across while reciting the Heart Sutra but collapsed after five days; woke on the night of the fifth day to a cool breeze and found a pond 4 kilometers ahead that saved his life; rested for one day by the pond → two days:

found three Chinese monks at a Buddhist temple in Yiwu ( 伊吾 , Hami); foreign monks and princes in the area came to visit him and pay their respects; the king of Hami invited him to a banquet; the banquet was also attended by an envoy from Gaochang ( 高昌國 ) who informed his king of Xuanzang’s arrival upon returning home →

six days in early 628: given a warm welcome by Qu Wentai ( 麴文泰 ), the king of Gaochang, who was of Chinese extraction; put up in a temple next to the palace; the king wanted Xuanzang to remain in Gaochang permanently, but Xuanzang refused and went on a hunger strike; the king allowed Xuanzang to depart on the condition that he explicate the Humane King Sutra for one month and stay for three years on his journey home → the king made four strong young men Buddhist monks and had them accompany Xuanzang and also provided funds for a 20-year return journey to India, including 30 robes, turbans, purses, shoes, 100 gold coins, 30,000 silver coins, and 500 bolts of silk; he also had an envoy accompany Xuanzang as far east as the Great Khan of the Western Turkic Khaganate, with letters for all the kings along the way asking for their cooperation
→ Aiqiniguo ( 阿耆尼國 , the town of Karasahr or Yanqi [ 焉耆 ] during the Han Dynasty, meaning “land of fire” in Sanskrit)

→ Quzhiguo ( 屈支國 , Guizi [ 龜玆 ]); the king, his courtiers, and the eminent monks received Xuanzang outside the east gate of the castle; he stayed there for two months; the king provided him with horses and camels
→ seven-day passage over Mount Ling ( 凌山 , north slope of Tian Shan), 4,286 meters above sea level and covered in perpetual snow
→ continued west along the south shore of Daqingchi ( 大淸池 , Issyk Kul, known as the “warm lake”)

→ Met the Turkish khan at his tents near the city of Suye (also called Suiye [ 碎葉 ], a trading center near mod- ern-day Tokmak); the khan invited him to a banquet, assigned him a young translator to guide him to the northwest border of India, gave him robes and silk, and provided him with letters of introduction to the kings along the way; the king and a number of his courtiers accompanied Xuanzang for the first 4 kilometers of his journey
→ Qianquan ( 千泉 , summer resort of the Turks)
→ city of Daluosi ( 呾邏私城 , Taraz, currently Dzhambul)
→ about 80 kilometers to the southwest: city of Baishui ( 白水城 , Sayram to the northeast of modern-day Tashkent)
→ about 80 kilometers to the southwest: city of Gongyu ( 恭御城 , around the Chirchik River and Angren River, tributaries of the Syr Darya)
→ south for around 20 kilometers: Nuchijianguo [ 笯赤建國 ], east of Tashkent)
→ west for about 80 kilometers: Zheshiguo ( 赭時國 , Shiguo, modern-day Tashkent)
→ southwest for about 400 kilometers: Shuaidulisenaguo ( , current Panjikand)

→ northwest across a desert for about 200 kilometers: Samojianguo ( 颯秣建國 , Samarkand, Kangguo [ 康國 ]); there were two Zoroastrian temples without any Buddhist monks; Buddhists were now allowed to stay here
→ southwest for about 120 kilometers: Jieshuangnaguo ( 羯霜那國 , Shiguo [ 史國 ], current Shakhrisabz)
→ south for 200– 240 kilometers: Tiemen ( 鐵門 , Buzgala, 36 kilometers south of Shakhrisabz)
→ 29 small kingdoms under the control of Duhuoluo ( 覩貨邏國 , Tocharistan)
→ crossed the Amu Darya River and reached Huoguo ( 活國 , near modern-day Kunduz, Afghanistan); stayed here for one month; the viceroy was the eldest son of the Turkish khan

→ spent one month at Doufuheguo ( 都縛喝國 , Bactria, west of modern-day Mazar-iSharif in north Afghanistan), a Buddhist country with 3,000 or 4,000 monks, and around 100 Buddhist temples; Xuanzang viewed three relics of Shakyamuni (a basin used when bathing, a comb, and a tooth) at Navasangharama ( ), a monastery southwest of the city
→ south for about 40 kilometers Jiezhiguo ( 揭職國 , current Darrah Gaz in Pakistan) → southeast: Mount Daxue ( 大雪山 , Hindu Kush mountains)

remained for 15 days in Fanyannaguo ( 梵衍那國 , modern-day Bamiyan south of Kabul, the capital of Afghanistan); there 率堵利瑟那國納縛僧伽藍 were thousands of monks and dozens of Buddhist temples, along with huge golden standing statues of the Buddha, about 12– 15 meters high on the slope of the mountain northeast of the royal capital
east: Heiling ( 黑嶺 , Mount Siyah Kah in eastern Afghanistan)
observed vassa at Shajia Temple ( 沙落迦寺 ) in Jiabishi ( 迦畢試 , modern-day Bagram, Afghanistan)

winter 628: Nanboguo ( 濫波國 , Lampaka, modern-day Laghman in eastern Afghanistan) in northern India; at the time, this marked the boundary of India; Xuanzang’s Great Tang Records on the Western Regions ( ) was the first place to refer to India as “Yindu,” the Chinese word for the modern country; the word “India” came from the word for river, and it originally only referred to the Indus River; in Sanskrit the word for India was “Sindhu”; in ancient China this was known as Shendu ( 身毒 ), Xiandou ( 賢豆 ), and Tianzhu ( 天竺 )
southeast: Jieluohe ( 揭羅曷 , Nagarahara, current Jalalabad in Afghanistan)
southeast: Jiantuoluoguo ( 健馱邏國 , Gandhara, its capital was Purusapura, west of modern-day Peshawar in Afghanistan)
northeast: city of Busejieluofadi ( 布 , Puskalavati, modern-day Charsadda, to the northeast of Peshawar)
east: city of Balusha ( 跋虜沙城 , Shahbaz Garhi, northeast of Peshawar)
east: Wuduojiahancha ( , Ohind, to the east of Peshawar)
north for about 240 kilometers: Wuzhangnaguo ( 烏仗那國 , Udyana, which is to say Wuchang [ 烏萇 ], in the Swat river valley in modernday Pakistan)
city of Mengjieli ( 甍揭釐城 , modern-day Mangalore in north Pakistan)
northeast for about 400 kilometers along the Indus River: Daliluo River ( 達麗羅江 , Darel River in northern Pakistan)
east for about 200 kilometers: Boluluoguo ( 鉢露羅國 , modern-day Baltistan in northwest Kashmir)
returned to Wuduojiahancha ( 烏鐸迦漢茶 )
crossing the Indus River to the south: Dachashiluo ( 呾叉始羅 , Taksasile, northwest of modern-day Rawalpindi, Pakistan)
crossing the Indus River to the north
southeast: Wulashiguo ( 烏剌尸國 , Urasa, near modern-day Hazara in northeast Pakistan)

southeast for about 400 kilometers: remained in Srinagar, capital of Jiashimiluoguo ( 迦濕彌羅國 , Kasmira, in the region of modern-day Kash- 大唐西域記色羯邏伐底城烏鐸迦漢茶 mir) for two years; on the orders of the king he discussed Buddhism with dozens of monks and took lectures on logic, linguistic and grammatical studies, and grammar
southwest for about 280 kilometers: Bannujieguo ( 半笯嗟國 , Parnotsa, modern-day Punch, southwest of Srinagar)
southeast for about 160 kilometers: Heluoshebuluoguo ( , Rajapura, modern-day Rajaori, Kashmir)
southeast: the capital of Zhejiaguo ( 磔迦國 , Takka, around the Punjab Plain); the capital of the Punjab in the seventh century was Asarur
east for about 200 kilometers: Napudiguo ( 那僕底國 , modern-day Kasur in northeast Pakistan or Firozpur in north India)
east: Shelandaluoguo ( , Jalamdhara, modern-day Jalandhar in north India)
northeast for about 280 kilometers: Guluduoguo ( 屈露多國 , Kuluta, modern-day Kangra in north India)
south for about 280 kilometers: Sheduotuluguo ( , Satadru, around modern-day Sirhind in north India); this section was north India
midThe 曷邏闍補羅國設多圖盧國闍爛達羅國 India, southwest for about 320 kilometers: Boliyedaluo ( , Pariyatra, modern-day Vairata, north of Jaipur, India)
east for about 200 kilometers: Motuluo ( 秣菟羅 , Marthura or Motouluo [ 摩頭羅 ], modern-day Mathura)
northeast for about 200 kilometers: Satanishifaluoguo ( , Sthanesvara, modern-day Thanesar)
east for about 160 kilometers: Suluqinaguo ( , Srughna, area north of modern-day Rohtak)
east after crossing the Jingjia River ( 殑伽江 , Ganga, Ganges River)
Modibuluoguo ( 秣底補羅國 , Matipura, modern-day Mandawar)
north for about 120 kilometers: Poluoximobuluo ( , Brahmapura, modern-day Srinagar)
spring 630, turned around: Modibuluoguo ( )
southeast for about 160 kilometers: Jupishuangnaguo ( 瞿毘霜那國 , Govisana, around modern-day Llfain, east of Kashipur)
southeast for about 160 kilometers: Exichetaluoguo ( , Ahichattra, east of modern-day Rohilkhand)
south for 104 or 108 kilometers and then southwest after crossing the Ganges River
Piluoshannaguo ( , Virasana or Bilsar)
southeast for about 80 kilometers: Jiebitaguo ( 劫比他國 , Kapitha, also called Sengjiashi [ 僧伽施 ], modern-day Sankisa village in Farrukhabad)

southeast for about 80 kilometer: Jieruojushe ( 羯若鞠闍 , Kanyakubja in Sanskrit, meaning “city of the hunchbacked maidens,” this is Jiaraoyi ( 罽饒夷 ) , from Record of Faxian ( 法顯傳 ) , modern-day Kanauj; at the time, northern and central India were ruled by Harshavardhana (r. 606– 647), or Harsha for short; the king’s nickname was Siladitya, meaning “wary of the sun”; there were around 10,000 monks in Jieruojushe and about 100 Buddhist temples; studied vibhasa with a Tripitaka master named Viryasena ( )
southeast for about 40 kilometers: city of Nafutipoluo (Navadevakula [ ], modern-day Oudh)
east for about 120 kilometers and north along the Ganges River by boat: ambushed by a pirate ship in Ayemuquguo ( , northwest of modernday Allahabad)
southeast for about 280 kilometers and then north after crossing the Yamuna River ( 閻牟那江 ): Boluoyegaguo ( , Prayaga, modern-day Allahabad)

southwest through a 波理夜呾羅薩他泥濕伐羅國窣祿勤那國婆羅吸摩補羅秣底補羅國惡醯掣呾邏國毘羅刪拏國納縛提婆矩羅城阿耶穆佉國毘離耶犀那鉢邏耶伽國 great forest for about 200 kilometers: Jiaoshangmiguo ( 憍賞彌國 , Kausambi, modern-day Kosam village, southwest of Allahabad)
northeast for 68 or 72 kilometers: Bingsuojiaguo ( 鞞索迦國 , Visaka, variously regarded as modern-day Oudh, Biseipur, or Sitapur)

northeast for about 200 kilometers: Shiluofaxideguo ( , Sravasti, two villages of Saheth and Maheth on the south shore of the Rapti River); city of Shewei ( 舍衛城 ) in the Juisaluoguo ( 拘薩羅國 ) from Record of Faxian ; Jetavananathapin-dikarama, also called Jetavana, was already in ruins

southeast for about 200 kilometers: Jiebiluofasuduguo ( , Kapilavastu, around the modern-day village of Nigliva in north India); the birthplace and hometown of the Buddha, Lanpini ( 藍毗尼 , Lumbini), was also in ruins
east for about 80 kilometers: Lanmoguo ( 藍摩國 , Rama)
northeast through a great forest: Jushinajieluoguo ( , Kusinagara), where the Buddha attained nirvana
southwest: Poluonisiguo ( , modern-day Varanasi)
east for about 120 kilometers along the Ganges River: Zhanzhuguo ( 戰主國 , meaning “warlord,” a translation rather than a transcription of the Sanskrit word Garjanapati, modern-day Ghazipur)
northeast along the Ganges River for about 60 kilometers: Feisheliguo ( 吠舍釐國 , Vaisali, modern-day Basarh)

east for about 200 kilometers: Fulishiguo ( 弗栗恃國 , Vrji, area north of modern-day Darbhanga); here Xuanzang was told that a country called Nipoluo ( 尼波羅國 , Nepala, modern-day Nepal) lay 560– 600 kilometers to the northwest
opposite direction: Feisheliguo ( 吠舍釐國 )

630: traveled south along the Ganges River: Majietuoguo ( 麻揭陀國 ); the ancient cities here including Balianfuyi ( 巴連弗邑 ), which was its capital at the time of Faxian’s visit, were all in ruins, but it retained its standing as the heart of Buddhism; made a pilgrimage to Putigaye ( 菩提伽倻 , Bodh Gaya), where the Buddha achieved enlightenment, and prostrated himself before the diamond throne and the bodhi tree

the monastery of Nalantuo (Nalanda [ 那爛陀 ]); the Gupta Empire built the Nalanda monastery (village of Baragoan inside Patna), the greatest Buddhist school in India, north of the city of Jiuwangshe ( 舊王室羅伐悉底國劫比羅伐窣堵國拘尸那揭羅國婆羅痆斯國 舍城 , Kusagrapura); at this point the monastery almodern-day ready had a 700-year history; while Xuanzang was visiting Bodh Gaya, a messenger was sent from the monastery to greet him; after finding a place to stay, Xuanzang visited holy Buddhist sites such as Griddhraj Parvat, or Vulture Peak, the place where the Buddha explicated the Flower Adornment Sutra, and Venuvana Monastery, the bamboo grove where he preached many of his sermons; studied Buddhist texts like A Compendium of Validities under Jiexian ( 戒賢 , Silabhadra), abbot of Nalanda, for 15 months, and learned about Yogacara, Shunzhengli ( 順正理 ), Xianyang ( 顯揚 ), Duifa ( 對法 ), logic, and linguistic and grammatical studies; became a disciple of Silabhadra; also was highly honored as one of the 10 most illustrious monks of the thousands of monks visiting the monastery
635, embarked on his tour of the five kingdoms of India: Yilannabofaduoguo ( , modern-day Monghyr in the state of Bihar, India); remained there for one year; studied vibhasa with two eminent monks
east for about 120 kilometers on the south coast of the Ganges River: Zhanpoguo ( 瞻婆國 , Campa, near modern-day Bhagalpur in east Bihar)
east for about 80 kilometers: Jiezhuwazhiluoguo ( , Kajunghira, modern-day Rajmahal in northeast India)
east and across the Ganges River, about 240 kilometers: Bennafadannaguo ( , Punnavardhana, around modern-day Bongra)
southeast for about 360 kilometers: Heluonasufalanaguo ( 褐羅 , Karnasuvarna, around modern-day Murshidabad)
southeast: Sanmotazhaguo ( 三魔呾吒國 , Samatata, around modern-day Gomilla, southwest of Dhaka); Xuanzang was told about six countries in Southeast Asia, corresponding to modern-day Myanmar and Thailand

west for about 360 kilometers: Danmolidiguo ( , Tamralipti, modern-day Tamluk in east India), Xuanzang was told about a country called Senggaluo ( 僧伽羅國 , Singhala, Shiziguo [ 獅子國 ], modern-day Sri Lanka) in the sea
southwest: Wuchaguo ( 烏茶國 , Udra, around modern-day Ganjam district of Odisha state)

southwest through great plains and a dense forest: Jielinggaguo ( 羯陵伽國 , Kalinga, great ancient kingdom on the east coast of India, around 伊爛拏鉢伐多國拏蘇伐剌那國羯朱嗢祇羅國奔那伐彈那國耽摩栗底國 Rajahmundry) on the boarder of south India
northwest for about 720 kilometers: Jiaosaluoguo ( 憍薩羅國 , Kosala, around modern-day Chanda) in central India
south for about 360 kilometers: Andaluoguo ( 案達羅國 , Andhra, around modern-day Hyderabad) in south India
south for about 400 kilometers: Tuonajiezhejiaguo ( 馱那羯磔迦國 , Dhanakataka, around modern-day Amaravati)
southwest for about 400 kilometers: Zhuliyeguo ( 珠利耶國 , Colya, around modern-day Nellore on the southeast coast of India)

south through the forest for 600 or 640 kilometers: Daluopichaguo ( , Dravida, the north part of the modern-day state of Tamil Nadu, south of the state of Andhra Pradesh); the initial plan was to continue south through the Moluojuzhaguo ( 秣羅矩吒國 , Malakuta, around modern-day Madura) and by sea to Senggaluoguo ( 僧伽羅國 , modern-day Sri Lanka); Xuanzang abandoned this plan when he heard from the king of Moluojuzha that various areas were troubled with war and famine; nevertheless, he heard a number of stories about Senggaluo
north: Gongjiannabuluoguo ( , Kongkanapura, Anegundi in modern-day southwest India)
northwest for 960 or 1,000 kilometers: Mohelatuoguo ( , Maharattha, around modern-day Nasik)
south for 400 kilometers and across the Naimotuo River ( 耐枺陀江 , Narmada River): Balujiechepoguo ( 跋祿羯呫婆國 , Bharukacchapa, modern-day Broach on the west coast of India)
southwest for about 800 kilometers: Molapoguo ( 摩臘婆國 , Malabar, around modern-day Ahmedabad)
northwest for three days: Qizhaguo ( 契吒國 , Kaira or Kutch)
north for 400 kilometers: Falapiguo ( 伐臘毘國 , Valabhi, northwest of modernday Bhavnagar)
northwest for about 280 kilometers: Anantuobuluoguo ( , Anandapura, Badnagar or Vadnagar)
turning around: Falapiguo
north for 720 kilometers: Juzheluoguo ( 瞿折羅國 , Gujjara, around the modern-day state of Gujarat)
southeast for about 1,120 kilometers: Wusheyannaguo ( , Ujjayani, around modern-day Ujjain in the state of Madhya Pradesh)
northeast for about 400 kilometers: Zhizhituo ( 擲枳陀 , Bundelkhand or Chitor)
north for about 360 達羅毗茶國摩訶剌陀國恭建那補羅國阿難陀補羅國鄔闍衍那國 kilometers: Moxishifaluobuluoguo ( 國 , Mahesvarapura, modern-day Gwalior in central India)
west: Sulatuo ( 蘇剌佗 , Saurashtra)
west: Aidianpoxueluoguo ( 阿點婆趐羅國 , around modern-day Karachi, Pakistan)

west for about 800 kilometers: Langjieluoguo ( 狼揭羅國 , Langala, around modern-day Lokorian in south-central Pakistan); Xuanzang was told that Bolasiguo ( 波剌斯 , Parsa [ 波斯 ], Bosi, Persia) lay to the northwest, with Fulinguo ( 拂菻國 , Frum, Rum, Eastern Roman Empire) to the west of that
reversing direction: Aidianpoxueluoguo
north for about 280 kilometers: Beiduoshiluoguo ( , Patasila, modern-day Hyderabad, Pakistan)
northeast for about 120 kilometers: Afanchaguo ( 阿軬茶國 , Avanda, modern-day Brahmanabad, Pakistan)
east for about 280 kilometers: Xinduguo ( 信度國 , Sindhu, around modern-day Sukkur, Pakistan)
east for about 360 kilometers, east after crossing the river: Maoluosanbuluguo ( , Mulasthanapura, around modern-day Multan in Pakistan), the border of west India

northeast for about 280 kilometers: Bofaduoguo ( 鉢伐多國 , Parvata, modern-day Harappa, Pakistan) in north India; resided here for two years. After extensive traveling through the five kingdoms of India— central, north, south, east, and west— Xuanzang traveled east from the Bofaduoguo and returned after two years to the Nalanda Monastery. On his return journey, he visited the Diluozejia Temple ( ) near Nalanda, where he studied Buddhist texts, along with linguistic and grammatical studies, and logic, from the monk Banruobatuoluo ( 般若跋陀羅 ). In addition, he went to Mount Zhanglin ( 杖林山 , on the east side of Mount Buddhain) to study Weishijeuzelun ( ) with the monk Jayasena. Upon returning to Nalanda, he explicated the Mahayanasamgraha and Weishijeuzelun to the monks of the temple. This is when Xuanzang wrote Treatise on the Harmony of Teaching ( 會宗論 ) in 3,000 stanzas, developing Yogacara and fusing it with the Madhyamika, or middle way, doctrines. After spreading his fame throughout India by participating in a religious convocation on the invitation of King Harsha, Xuanzang made up his mind to return home. His 摩醯濕伐羅補羅臂多勢羅國茂羅三部盧國唯識決擇論低羅擇伽寺 return journey is described below. Fall 641: departed from Boluoyega
southwest through the forest: Jiaoshangmi ( )
Piluoshanna ( )
Shelandaluo ( 達羅國 )
Senghebuluoguo ( )
Dachashiluoguo ( )
Lanboguo ( 覽波國 ); until this point Xuanzang was retracing his steps, but from this point on he chose a different route
south for 15 days: Falanaguo ( 伐剌拏國 , Varnu, Bana [ 跋那 ] in Faxian Zhuan, modern-day Bannu, Pakistan) in west India; paid respects at Buddhist sites
northwest: Aibojianguo ( 阿博建國 , modern-day Waziristan, west of Bannu)
northwest, departing India: Caojuzhaguo ( 漕矩吒國 , Jaguda, around modern-day Ghazni, Afghanistan)
north for about 200 kilometers: Fulishisatangnaguo ( , Vrjisthana, west of modern-day Kabul, Afghanistan)
east: Jiabishiguo ( 迦畢試國 )
snow-covered mountains, Khawak Pass in the Hindu Kush mountains: Andaluofuguo ( 安呾邏縛國 , Andarab, around the modern-day Doshi River west of Khawak Pass)
northwest for about 160 kilometers: Kuoxiduoguo ( 闊悉多國 , Khost river valley in northeast Afghanistan)

northwest: stayed in Huaguo ( ) for one month; learned here that Qu Wentai ( ), king of Gaochang, had died and that the kingdom of Gaochang had fallen (May 640); decided not to follow the north bank of the Amu Darya to Samarkand and then south of the Tian Shan ( 天山 ) Mountains; instead, chose the more difficult route along the south bank of the river and over the Pamir Mountains
east for about 40 kilometers: Mengjianguo ( 瞢健國 , modern-day Khan Abad, Afghanistan)
east for about 120 kilometers: Qilisemoguo ( , west of modern-day Fayzabad, Afghanistan)
east for about 80 kilometers: Boduochuangnaguo ( 鉢鐸創那國 , around modern-day Jurm in Afghanistan)
southeast for about 200 kilometers: Qulangnaguo ( 屈浪拏國 , modern-day Quran on the upper Kokcha River)
northeast for around 200 kilometers: Damoxitie Empire ( 達摩悉鐵帝國 , around modern-day Wakhan, Afghanistan)
northeast for about 280 kilometers: Bomiluo River ( 波謎羅川 , modern-day Pamil)
southeast for about 200 毘羅刪拏國呾叉始羅國憍賞彌國闍爛僧訶補羅國弗栗恃薩儻那國滑國麴文泰訖栗瑟摩國 kilometers: Qiepantuoguo ( 朅盤陀国 , modern-day Tashkurgan [ ], Xinjiang [ 新疆 ])
Qusha ( 佉沙 , Kashgar, Shulei [ 疎勒 ])
Zhujubo ( 朱俱波 , Shache [ 莎車 ])

644: east for about 320 kilometers: there were around 5,000 monks and 100 Buddhist temples at Qusadanna ( 瞿薩旦那 , Hotan); Xuanzang sent Maxuanzhi ( 馬玄智 ), a young man from Gaochang, as an envoy to Chang’an to inform the Tang court that he was returning from his pilgrimage to India; after eight months, the messenger returned with a reply from Emperor Taizong ( 太宗 ) of Tang; the emperor said he was overjoyed to learn of Xuanzang’s return and wanted to meet him in person; dispatched people to Kucha ( 龜玆 ) and Kashgar to replace the Buddhist scriptures they had lost on the road; lectured to about a thousand monks every day
east to Niya
around 440 kilometers across a great desert: the old site of Zhemotuona ( 折摩馱那 )
northeast for about 400 kilometers: ruins of Nafubo ( 納縛波 , Loulan [樓欄 ])
November 644: Shazhou ( 沙州 , Dunhuang [ 敦煌 ])
January 6, 645: traveled by boat along a canal and reached Chang'an.
